title: Backup power for grid outages
subtitle: Comparing UPS units, portable LiFePO4 batteries, and petrol generators for household resilience
excerpt: UK grid outages last on average under 50 minutes for planned works and under 3 hours for faults. Resilience planning divides into two tiers: lights and device charging (a 500 Wh LiFePO4 battery handles this for 12-24 hours) and critical appliances like medical equipment or a refrigerator (requires a larger system or a generator).
sourceNotes: Ofgem Guaranteed Standards of Performance for power cuts (DG3 payment rights, 3-hour and 18-hour thresholds). National Grid ESO outage statistics for England and Wales 2022-23. IET Code of Practice for Electrical Energy Storage Systems. LiFePO4 battery chemistry safety documentation (UL 9540A). Which? generator buying guide 2023.

glossaryTerms.definition: Lithium iron phosphate: a lithium battery chemistry that is thermally stable, long-cycle-life (2,000-4,000 cycles), and safer than other lithium chemistries for enclosed household use.
Kilowatt-hour: one unit of electrical energy. A 500 Wh (0.5 kWh) battery powers a 50 W LED light for 10 hours or charges a phone from 0-100% around 40 times.

content.text: UK power outages are short by international standards. Most unplanned faults resolve within 3 hours. Backup power in the UK is less about extended survival and more about: keeping food safe in the fridge-freezer, running medical equipment (CPAP, refrigerated medication), keeping lights on, and charging phones.
Tier 1: lighting and device charging (500-1000 Wh battery)

portable power station (e.g., Jackery Explorer 500, EcoFlow River 2) powers: 4 LED bulbs (40W total) for 12 hours, phone charging 40 times, laptop charging 8 times, and a small fan for 10 hours. Cost: a modest cost. Recharges from the mains in 4-8 hours. Can also be recharged from a solar panel (100 W panel replenishes in 5-8 hours of summer sunshine). These units use
-scale batteries with overcharge and thermal protection. They produce no fumes. No installation required.
Tier 2: refrigerator and medical equipment (2000-5000 Wh)

A fridge-freezer uses 100-200 W running (but not continuously: typically 30-50% duty cycle). To run it for 12 hours requires 600-1,200 Wh. A CPAP machine uses 30-100 W depending on the model; 8 hours requires 240-800 Wh. A 2,000 Wh LiFePO4 system covers both comfortably for 12 hours. Cost: a modest cost. These larger units (EcoFlow Delta, Bluetti AC200P) also support solar panel input for extended operation.
Tier 3: petrol generator for extended outages

A petrol inverter generator (2-3 kW output) powers a fridge, lights, and device charging simultaneously. Cost: a modest cost. Running cost at current fuel prices: approximately 50p-80p per hour on unleaded. Petrol generators must ONLY be used outdoors or in a ventilated outhouse: they produce carbon monoxide and must not run in enclosed spaces under any circumstances. A 10-litre petrol can gives approximately 8-12 hours of running time.

A UPS is a small battery that sits between the mains and a device and switches over instantly when power fails. It protects computers and routers from data loss during brief outages or voltage spikes. A 1500 VA UPS (around a modest cost) keeps a desktop computer running for 15-20 minutes, long enough for an orderly shutdown. It is not designed for extended runtime; it is specifically for the 0-15 minute gap while you switch to a generator or battery system.
